summary: Joel Spolsky excoriates Travelers Insurance for running an ad in Inc. Magazine that stereotypes geeks as threats and criminals. The ad depicted a poorly-dressed person and warned business owners about 'high-tech criminals,' implying geeks are dangerous. Spolsky argues the ad is offensive on multiple levels: it demonizes the people who build technology, it reflects high-school bully mentality, and it insults the very audience reading Inc. Magazine. He points out the irony of insurance salesmen claiming to protect against geeks, and concludes by announcing he's taking his business elsewhere.
key_points: The ad perpetuates harmful stereotypes by equating geeks with criminals and threats to business
Insurance salesmen mocking geeks is ironic given their own actuaries are geeks, and they're unqualified to 'protect' anyone from technical threats
The ad fundamentally misreads its audience, since Inc. Magazine readers are largely the tech founders and geeks being mocked
Anti-intellectual attitudes have become normalized, extending from politics into corporate advertising
Consumers who feel insulted by a company's advertising will take their business elsewhere
money_quotes: Am I nuts, or is this ad really offensive?
What is this, high school? With the bullies who fail all their classes have such an inferiority complex they have to make fun of the geeks?
I can't really think of anyone less qualified to 'protect' you from 'geeks' than a bunch of insurance salesmen.
Have you looked at your own actuaries? Have you noticed how insurance people dress?
Can you imagine anyone more harmless than a geek?

Maybe the current Bush presidency has given you the idea that it's ok to make fun of the scientists, inventors, researchers and programmers who are creating the future, finding cures for your diseases, building the spreadsheets you use to figure out how much commission you're making.
You probably forgot that most of the people that read Inc. are geeks. And we buy insurance. Lots of insurance.
And in fact I used to buy it from you. But not any more.
key_insight: Companies that insult their own customers in advertising deserve to lose them, and mocking the technical people who build the modern world reflects a deeply misguided anti-intellectual culture.
